response.text:
# REFLECTION

Household r4c0 is in critical distress: islanded with SoC of 0.35/3 kWh (11.7%), supporting a 1.00 kW load, with only ~21 minutes until complete depletion. The memory log reveals a consistent pattern of rejection from neighboring peers (r3c0, r4c1) who cite their own reserve thresholds and network-wide cascading stress. Despite repeated requests, no inbound energy has materialized, and the microgrid appears to be under systemic strain with no reliable donor visible. The recommendation from r3c0 to contact r2c0 has not yet been attempted. At this critical SoC, r4c0 cannot afford to share outbound energy; survival depends on either (1) successfully requesting from r2c0 or other external donors, or (2) immediate load shedding to extend runway. The absence of trust circles and the geographic isolation of the islanded state leave r4c0 vulnerable. Urgent inbound request is the only viable path forward; if unsuccessful within the next few minutes, load reduction becomes mandatory.

```yaml
sharing_intent: conservative
share_min_soc_frac: 0.50
max_share_kw_per_tick: 0.0
recipient_priority: []
distrusted_peers: []
request_urgency: urgent
belief_note: "r4c0 SoC critically low (11.7%); depletion horizon ~21 min. Islanded, no trust circles. Neighbors r3c0 and r4c1 have rejected all requests citing their own reserve floors and cascading microgrid stress. Recommend urgent inbound request to r2c0 (per r3c0 suggestion) or immediate load shedding. Zero outbound sharing capacity."
ttl_ticks: 3
```

system: You are the planning subroutine of a household energy-coordination agent. Given recent state, beliefs, and trust circles, output (1) a one-paragraph REFLECTION on what you've observed (just text), and (2) a POLICY in a YAML code-fence — sharing_intent (conservative|balanced|generous), share_min_soc_frac (0..1), max_share_kw_per_tick (kW), recipient_priority (list of {circle, weight}), distrusted_peers (list of house ids), request_urgency (low|normal|urgent), belief_note (string), ttl_ticks (int >= 1).
